song_info: In "Ozbak" [17] the only words that come out clearly are: 'Ozbak is our hero', although every singer knows the legend behind the song. Ozbak, according to this legend, managed to overcome grief after his son had been killed. He did not take revenge on those who killed his son, because he believed that his son was to blame. The heroism of the father is in his high sense of justice. The song ends with a dance to praise the hero for his high moral. //ON ALEXANDER STREET, IN THE LINER NOTE OF THE ALBUM: The Golden Fleece - Caucasus, Vol. 2: Songs From Abkhazia & Adzharia, PAGE 7//

song_info: This song is a traditional tune from the repertoire of the lashuar, very short, communal songs that used to be played at family gatherings. It talks about a beduin region in Mauritania named Bamuera. The poet loves a woman from the region and writes about his feelings. The performance occasion was a private session at the cultural centre of Ausserd camp. Mariem Mouloud, expert in traditional music, was playing the traditional tbal drum. Traditional Saharawi music was always played with only voice and drum. It's very quiet music, to be able to listen to the words. //BRITISH LIBRARY, SHELF MARK C1640/79, VIOLETA RUANO PORTRAITS OF SAHARAWI MUSIC, I HAVE TO VISIT BAMUERA, ABSTRACT//

song_label: Love songs //BRITISH LIBRARY, VIOLETA RUANO PORTRAITS OF SAHARAWI MUSIC, GENRE//
song_info: This song is a modern interpretation of a traditional love song from the repertoire of the lashuar, very short, communal songs that used to be played at family gatherings. In it, a man expresses his feelings for a woman.The performance occasion was a rehearsal of the government musicians of the Ausserd camp. The recording was made in the cultural centre, where there were a lot of people coming in and out. The band structure voice, electric guitar and electronic keyboard is the most common one for the performance of Saharawi music nowadays. The singer was playing the traditional tbal drum. //BRITISH LIBRARY, SHELF MARK C1640/76, VIOLETA RUANO PORTRAITS OF SAHARAWI MUSIC, BIB'S DAUGHTER, ABSTRACT//

song_info: This song is a traditional tune from the repertoire of the lashuar, very short, communal songs that used to be played at family gatherings. It talks about a man whose wife has abandoned him.The performance occasion was a private session at the cultural centre of Ausserd camp. Mariem Mouloud, expert in traditional music, was playing the traditional tbal drum. Traditional Saharawi music was always played with only voice and drum. It's very quiet music, to be able to listen to the words. //BRITISH LIBRARY, SHELF MARK C1640/77, VIOLETA RUANO PORTRAITS OF SAHARAWI MUSIC, ABSTRACT//

song_label: NA
song_info: This song is a traditional tune from the repertoire of the lashuar, or shadow music, which was performed during the day, when the sun still makes shadows. It talks about a tree named teiduma. Somebody loves a woman that he encounters under a tree. When he comes back to the same spot, she is not there anymore and the tree is dead. These songs were usually performed at family or other social events. The performance occasion was a private session organised by the musician in her house. Tarba Bueibu normally performs at national events with the national opera, but she does not consider herself a professional musician. She was playing a plastic bucket as a drum. //BRITISH LIBRARY SHELF MARK: C1640/55, COLLECTION: VIOLETA RUANO PORTRAITS OF SAHARAWI MUSIC, CATEGORY: LOVE SONGS, TITLE: TEIDUMA, ABSTRACT //

song_name: Ddikr pour le dépiquage des Ida Oumahmoud
song_reason: The song is identified as such in its title or label, There is text in associated ethnography that states what type of song this is (this could be in liner notes, it could be in an email from an anthropologist, etc)
song_label: Ddikr pour le dépiquage des Ida Oumahmoud [= Dhikr for threshing from the Ida Ou Mahmoud tribe] //CREM CNRSMH_E_1997_006_001_03 TITLE//
song_info: Il s'agit du ddikr chanté pour le début du dépiquage alors que les bêtes commencent à tourner sur l'aire. Deux hommes chantent en alternance, l'un faisant tourner les bêtes, l'autre ramenant très vite entre chaque passage des animaux les gerbes sur l'aire afin qu'elles soient piétinées. Les paroles chantées constituent les 3 premières strophes du ddikr noté et traduit p. 45. On note l'importance des cris des hommes aux bêtes et les braiments des ânes dans cette partie du dépistage très éprouvante pour les hommes et les bêtes. [= This is the dhikr sung for the beginning of the threshing as the animals begin to turn on the area. Two men sing alternately, one turning the animals, the other quickly bringing sheaves on the area between each passage of the animals so that they are trampled. […] The importance of the cries of men to the animals and the braying of the donkeys is noted in this part of the screening [?] which is very trying for men and animals.] //CREM CNRSMH_E_1997_006_001_03 ETHNOGRAPHIC CONTEXT//
song_morenotes: The WorldCat link refers to a book; inside that book there is an attached CD that contains the track.
morenotes: The CREM link is <http://archives.crem-cnrs.fr/archives/items/CNRSMH_E_1997_006_001_03/>, but the audio is unavailable there.

song_name: Ddikr pour le vannage des Ida Oumahmoud
song_reason: The song is identified as such in its title or label, There is text in associated ethnography that states what type of song this is (this could be in liner notes, it could be in an email from an anthropologist, etc)
song_label: Ddikr pour le vannage des Ida Oumahmoud [= Dhikr for winnowing of the Ida Ou Mahmoud] //CREM CNRSMH_E_1997_006_001_04 TITLE//
song_info: Profitant du vent soufflant dans la bonne direction, quelques femmes lancent en l'air avec leur fourche l'orge broyée. Le vent emporte la paille plus loin et laisse tomber le grain. Le ddikr chanté en antiphonie comporte les paroles suivantes : Prière de Dieu sur le Prophète / Qu'il intercède pour nous lors du jugement dernier. [= Taking advantage of the wind blowing in the right direction, several women throw crushed barley in the air with their fork. The wind carries the chaff farther away and lets the grain fall. The dhikr, sung antiphonally, contains the following words: Prayer of God on the Prophet / May he intercede for us at the last judgment.] //CREM CNRSMH_E_1997_006_001_04 ETHNOGRAPHIC CONTEXT//

song_label: "NA"
song_info: Over the centuries, the Khmer people have believed in natural spirits, souls (pralung), territorial spirits (neak ta), and all that preserves and protects the earth. Traditionally, these spirits and forces have been respected and feared. Arek has been traditionally performed in order to serve the needs of these spiritual forces and show them continual gratitude. When someone is sick, Arek music is played to call the spirit to come, entering a person who is then asked the reasons for the illness. Once the sick person is healthy again, the Laing roang ceremony is performed in which all the protective spirits are invited for feast and to listen to festive music. Offerings of food and drink, along with Arek music, are offered to the spirits in times when there is no rain, if animals or humans in the region are struck by disease, or when a large and important undertaking is planned. These ceremonies ask the spirits for help to solve a problem or to fulfill a wish or desired plan.

song_label: Praise //WORLDCAT IDENTIFIER: 4973632905//
song_info: One might plausibly assert that the Melanesians of the western Torres Strait have moved along with the world, turning to new cultural practices and losing interest in the traditions perceived as old and outdated once Western ideas and technologies reached the islands’ shores. The liner notes explain that most of the songs and dances on this recording are documentations of a now extinct tradition that largely disappeared around the turn of the twentieth century, in fact asserting that this recording may have been the last time many of the songs were ever performed. This aside, the legends and folk tales woven into chants and drumming provide a compelling look into the past. //ABSTRACT ON WORLDCAT IDENTIFIER: 4973632905//

song_label: MEN 'S NARRATIVE SONG //SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, UNES08027_112//
song_info: The 'ai ni mae are narrative songs with texts that relate myths and historical traditions. These narrations, some of which last for several hours, are divided into different sections. Each of which has a particular melody. Each melody is sung several times while the words follow their course. The story told in the present example is about a young man called Molea who, on a dark night, by mistake entered the sacred garden of a young girl named Lalama, who obliges him to marry her.//SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, UNES08027_112//

song_info: “While visiting a friendly king, King Solomon is asked about his family. Ashamed of having only three daughters, he resorts to a lie, pretending to have three sons. His host now asks the Jewish king to send him one of them. Much distressed. Solomon returns home, deciding to send him one of his daughters disguised as a man. In order to test his daughters courage, he dresses up as a robber and waits in ambush for each of them. The youngest passes the test well and is thus chosen to travel to the foreign court.” //AWM CD 12367 IN LOEB; INFO IN LINER NOTES//

song_info: “A native of Ste-Engrace returns home with smuggled Cheese from Spain. A thunderstorm makes him seek refuge in a cave. where he meets the “wild man”, the devil. He tells the wanderer that he will devour him unless he tells him three truths — whereupon the other: 1. They say that a full moon night is as light as day, but I do not believe in this. 2. They say that maize bread is better than tasty wheat bread, but I do not believe in this. 3. If I had known that I would meet you here I would not have come. The wild man agrees to these three truths and only asks the native of Ste—Engrace never to come into his sight again.” //AWM CD 12367 IN LOEB; INFO IN LINER NOTES//

song_reason: The song is identified as such in its title or label, There is text in associated ethnography that states what type of song this is (this could be in liner notes, it could be in an email from an anthropologist, etc)
song_label: Title: Funeral Song For Selikian Sub Genre: Song; Ritual; Funeral Music //ALEXANDER STREET CATALOG NUMBER: 4005 KCD//
song_info: February 17, 1967 little Selikian died. She was a mentally and physically retarded Down syndrome child. Her parents, her father in particular, were very fond of her and took touchingly care of her. A week after her death the first mortuary feast we held. This feast is traditionally called omanganon. In the past it was probably seen as a means to establish the soul in the hereafter. Many people were there, and suddenly one of the men took over the kalawasi. As he explained in his song he took the place of his old mother, who was a classificatory grandmother of the child. She should have been present but was too ill to come. She had asked her son to sing in her place. //LINER NOTES; ALEXANDER STREET CATALOG NUMBER: 4005 KCD//
song_lyrics: you did not inform me [that you were ill], daughter/girl you are very sad, little daughter I want to come, your grandmother wants to come but she can't you must make, you must make this [song, the old woman had told her son] I want to go but I cannot do so I am ill, my child, I am very ill my child mother, don't be angry [the singer shouts at the women in the circle who at first refused to join the singing - now they start repeating his lines] I am ill, my child, I am very ill my child it is present, the sounds of the flute [kwama] is present [i.e. I can sing] //LINER NOTES; ALEXANDER STREET CATALOG NUMBER: 4005 KCD//

song_info: le chant du retour des Momaï au village; "more exactly the song of the return of the Momaï to the village"//ETHNOGRAPHIC CONTEXT ON CREM CODE: CNRSMH_I_2000_004_014_04//
morenotes: According to the Appendix of Wayana languages, "momai" are "neophyte(s) in maraké," and "maraké" is "Wayana ritual performance/stinging ritual." This song is thus for the return of the momai from a Wayana ritual. (http://www.academia.edu/8488647/Appendix_Wayana_Language_some_basics_)

song_label: NA
song_info: La bonne orthographe du titre est "Këlëkë jekë", il s'agit d'un chant pour encourager les Moma ; "The correct spelling of the title is "Këlëkë jekë", it is a song to encourage the Momaïs." //ETHNOGRAPHIC CONTEXT ON CREM CODE: CNRSMH_I_2000_004_014_05//
morenotes: According to the Appendix of Wayana languages, "momai" are "neophyte(s) in maraké," and "maraké" is "Wayana ritual performance/stinging ritual." The "eki" are "1) relatives. 2) pet animal. 3) supporter to momai during maraké." This song is thus sung by relatives/supporters to encourage the momai for the maraké ritual.(http://www.academia.edu/8488647/Appendix_Wayana_Language_some_basics_)

song_label: Aquanusa - Healing Chant //This is the title//
song_info: Important vocal elements are practiced by the men, such as the orations or prayers executed by the Neles or shamans to summon the help of the benevolent spirits to aid in the process of healing... Another important prayer is sung by the Nele for a pregnant woman when she is having pains or a difficult birth. //THE TEXT CAN BE FOUND IN THE LINER NOTE FOR THE ALBUM: Music of the Indians of Panama: The Cuna (Tule) and Chocoe (Embera) Tribes, WHICH IS ON ALEXANDER STREET//

song_label: Mournful song //CD LINER NOTES, AWM 19491//
song_info: A mournful song sung by the bereaved women after a qivittoq. In this song a direct influence (or continuation) of East-Greenlandic melody and form can be seen. This is also the case in many older South-Greenlandic songs of the niperujuatit tradition. But in the course of ti e a gradual incorporation of European melodics has taken place. The beginning European influence appears first in the use of semitone intervals, as in the present song. An elderly man is snoring in the background. //CD LINER NOTES, AWM 19491//

song_label: Singing game //CD LINER NOTES, LOEB AWM 19491//
song_info: A song for a singing game recorded in connection with a twin-town arrangement in Gentofte, Denmark. The singing game is about a hunting scene on a rocky coast. The girls stand facing each other and alternately clap their own and each other's hands. In an ever-accelerating tempo and with great precision — and volubility — the two girls keep pace with each other all through the song. This example shows that the East-Greenlandic song traditions are still in use, also among young people. //CD LINER NOTES, LOEB AWM 19491//

I Oh, you are so pitiful ! I Oh, you are so pitiful ! I From the shore we are going to throw you back into the ocean. I Where are your ears, Aloomit ? Can you not hear ? I Then, suddenly, the Aloomit pricked their ears and they were cast back into the ocean, although they did not wish to return to the ocean, for they intended to make their home on land ! //SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS LINER NOTES, CATALOG # UNES08053_115//

song_label: Ritual song; dance song; chant //CODING ID 1225 GENRE FROM GLOBAL JUKEBOX//
song_info: Kunwinjku land lies in the area famous for traditional Aboriginal rock art. Artists belong to clans and visually represent the ancestral creation stories or ‘Dreamings’ of their clan. Each clan has an estate where they hunt and gather edible plants and other necessities. Ancestral beings are believed to have created the features of the land. Spirits that animate an individual’s life reside there and at their death the spirit goes back to a Dreaming place.//CODING ID 1225 IN GLOBAL JUKEBOX//

song_name: Zaaj Tshoob - Ceeb Toom Nam Txiv (Announcement To The Parents Of The Groom)
song_reason: The song is identified as such in its title or label, There is text in associated ethnography that states what type of song this is (this could be in liner notes, it could be in an email from an anthropologist, etc)
song_label: Wedding Song - Announcement To The Parents Of The Groom // SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS CATALOG NUMBER ARH00446_108 //
song_info: The parents of the bride and the groom each arrange for a representative to facilitate...communications leading up to...the three-day ceremony with feasts at both at the villages of both families. A third legal agentcoordinates the proceedings of the ceremony itself. All of this is conducted in song. ln the two pieces presented here, the gravity of the marriage commitment is relayed by the symbols of the bull and of the umbrella, representing respectively the elemental power of the instinct for procreation and the security inherent in married life and ancestral blessing // SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS LINER NOTES
song_morenotes: CD name The Music of the Hmong People of Laos
morenotes: Not necessarily used in procession; used as part of the larger set of wedding rituals. More info necessary to determine the categorization.

song_reason: The song is identified as such in its title or label, There is text in associated ethnography that states what type of song this is (this could be in liner notes, it could be in an email from an anthropologist, etc)
song_label: "Song of the Alfereces (colour-bearers) and Dances of the Chinos (servants of the divinities and saints)" //LINER NOTES ASSOCIATED WITH THE ALBUM, PAGE 9//
song_info: In the provinces of Valparaiso, Tarapaca, Atacama, Coquimbo and Aconcagua there are brotherhoods which go by the general name of bailes (dancers). Their principal activity lies in paying obeisance, generally in the form of choreographic performances, to the catholic divinities and saints. One of the brotherhoods of this kind is that of the chinos (Chinese), a designation that has nothing to do with the inhabitants of China. The word chinos is in fact of Amerindian, of Quechua origin. It means «servant» and entered the Spanish language with the same signification but with reference to the observance of worshipping supernatural beings. By as early as the 17th century the activities of these brotherhoods had acquired a certain repute since they had become an important part of various religious festivals. The leader of a group of chinos dancers is called an alferez (colour-bearer), a reflection of the title given at the time of the Spanish conquest to the officer, who carried the standard or banner. //LINER NOTES ASSOCIATED WITH THE ALBUM, PAGES 9 and 10//

song_label: Dance and Festivity //CD HAS LINER NOTES BOOKLET, THIS IS ON PAGE 5//
song_info: The cueca is the national dance par excellence of Chile, where it is very widely performed. It has an historical and social significance and is also a vehicle of patriotic sentiment. It is danced by couples, who hold silk scarves. The cueca version selected for the present recording comprises the usual sequence of three parts which make up the complete development of the dance and are called pies (feet). The theme of the three parts is a love poem sung by two voices on the same basic melody. The harp accompaniment, which is supported by the strokes with the fingers executed by Juan Negrete on the resonator of the instrument, displays a very sober technique which is well in keeping with the style of the traditional Chilean harpists. //CD HAS LINER NOTES BOOKLET, THIS IS ON PAGE 5//

song_info: This is a song for a circle dance in which the lyrics are enacted and sung by all the participants except for t17. second verse which is sung by the boy and the last verse, sung h, the girl. To the first verse the dancers do a running step in a circle around a girl who holds a red ribbon in her hand. She ties the ribbon, a symbol of love and marriage, around the wrist of a boy whom she chooses from the ring of dancers. The boy enters the center of the circle and teasingly asks the girl to Ioosen the ribbon. She obliges and he immediately runs away out of the ring and in the opposite direction of the dancers He comes back to the center again and sings alone the last line of the fourth verse. Thereafter, the boy and girl dance together clasping both hands and turning with a springy running step while the girl sings the last verse in a slightly faster tempo than the previous verses. //SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, Swedish Folk Songs and Ballads, CATALOG NUMBER FW08819_202//

song_info: [...] "In re sponse to the Margrave's bidding the lady Beatrice urged the knight to be of good cheer and to sing them a song. He rose and to the melody of the Estampida they had just heard, he extemporised. The song survives and is the first example on our next record. It illustrates the strong and decisive influence of the popular dance music on the melodies of the Troubadours." //CATALOG NUMBER FW03700 / FT 3700 IN FOLKWAYS; RELEVANT INFO IN PAGE 4 OF LINER NOTES//
song_lyrics: Neither the Mayfeast Nor the leaves of the beech Nor the song of the birds, Nor the flowers of iris Are what please me, Gracious, joyful lady, Until a speedy messenger arrives From you, fair lady, to give me New pleasure, which love brings me And joy, That I approach you, true lady. And may he fall wounded, The jealous one, before he drives me away from her. //CATALOG NUMBER FW03700 / FT 3700 IN FOLKWAYS; RELEVANT INFO IN PAGE 4 OF LINER NOTES//
morenotes: "In Moorish Spain arose a great cultivation of 9.nd attention to Art. The origin of this influence is to be found in late Classic Antiquity and Persian roots, and it spread over all Christian Spain and the neighbour- ing territory of Southern France (Provence)." //CATALOG NUMBER FW03700 / FT 3700 IN FOLKWAYS; TEXT IN LINER NOTES//

song_lyrics: "When first to this country a stranger I came I placed my affection on a maid that was young; She being young and tender, her waist small and slender, Kind Nature had formed her for my overthrow. 2 On the banks of the Bann there where I first beheld her She appeared like great Juno, that fair Grecian queen; Her eyes shone like diamonds or stars brightly shining, Her cheeks bloomed like roses or blood drops on snow. 3 It was her cruel parents that first caused our variance Because she was rich and above my degree, But I’ll do my endeavour to gain my love’s favour The more she is come of a rich family. 4 Had I all the money that lies in the Indies Or all the bright diamonds that’s in the queen’s store I would spend it in shining diamonds for to deck you, my darling, For there’s no other charmer on earth I adore. 5 Now since I have gained her I’ll be contented for ever, I’ll put rings on her fingers and gold in her ears, And here on the lovely banks of the bonny Bann water In all sorts of pleasure I’ll reside with my dear." //IRISH TRADITIONAL MUSIC ARCHIVE, THE BANKS OF THE BANN SONG, RELEVANT TEXT IS IN THE LYRICS FIELD//

song_lyrics: The ships head 'north, the wind is fair, Sun is rising and in the high poop Stands Erlingur Skjalgsson of Jadar Scanning the sea to the south and thinking: "Why tarries Olafr Trygvasnri 7" Fifty were the galleys drifting, Sails all folded. Then, looking at the sea They said, these warriors brave: "Who has news of The Serpent? Why tarries 101afr Trygvason?" As appeared the red glow of dawning next day On the far horizon: no ship yet was sighted Once more like a rising gale resounded: "Who has news of The Serpent? Why tarries Olafr Trygvasoin?" Silence, silence then fell upon all As by each of the ships the icy waves Their lament made known: "On the Serpent man' is no more And Olafr Trygvason is lost!" Sitnce then the sea by Norway resounds, Especially at daybreak: "On The Serpent man is no more And Olafr Trygvason is lost!" //SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, The Icelandic Singers, CATALOG NUMBER MON00585_107//

song_label: "Liron, Liron"//Liner Notes from the album "Children's Songs and Games from Ecuador, Mexico, and Puerto Rico" by Smithsonian Folkways
song_info: "This game is a variant of A La Vibora de La Mar, the Spanish equivalent of London Bridge. Two platers, holdings hands high, join hands to form an arc. Before the game begins, each one selects a secret name. The children pass through the arc one at a time as the song is sung. On the last line one player is detained. The two secret names are whispered to him. He selects one and lines up behind the appropriate person. The game proceeds until everyone has been 'captured'. The game ends with a tug of war"//Liner Notes from the album "Children's Songs and Games from Ecuador, Mexico, and Puerto Rico" by Smithsonian Folkways

song_lyrics:
Solo: Kristinn Hallsson And it was Olav Trygvason Sailing o'er the North Sea wide, Bearing the hope to found a kingdom Far on the other side. Yonder the cliffs appearing Like a battlement loom, their dark crest rearing. And it was Olav Trygvason, Found he ''e'er a spot to land, E'en like the waves the royal desire Shattered upon the strand. "See," cried the bard upleaping, "Yonder snowcovered peaks o'er cloudbanks peeping And it was Olav Trygvason, Suddenly he seem'd to sight Towering temples, domes and spires, Glist'niog in virgin white. Then vow'd the King undaunted, With his followers to tread that land enchanted. Onward he went, the rushing streams Heralded the coming spring, Swayed the stormy wind, The forest strangely was murmuring, Sounds as of church bells chiming, And then spake the King, spake as tho' dreaming: "Here the spot to found our Kingdom. Hell! these temple walls defy thee! Hearts are swelling, souls are yearning, God to Thee alan'e be glory! Be, my faith, as yonder mountains, Root as deeply, shine as purely, And as these my faith strive upward On to Him, the AllACreator!" Olav's prayer be ours to utter, As we near the Throne of Mercy. Hearts are swelling, souls are yearning, God to Thee alone be glory! Be, my faith, as yonder motklitains, Root as deeply, shine as purely, And as these my soul shine upward, On to Him the All-Creator! On to God! 
//SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, The Icelandic Singers, CATALOG NUMBER MON00585_206//

song_name: VIER FRANSJES OP DE BAAN (Four French- men on the Highway)
song_reason: The song is identified as such in its title or label, There is text in associated ethnography that states what type of song this is (this could be in liner notes, it could be in an email from an anthropologist, etc)
song_label: NA
song_info: "A song from Flanders, sung at fairs. It tells us the story of a noble lady who, returning with her servant from the town of Namen to her father's castle, is assaulted by four French highwaymen. At the crucial moment -- the scoundrels have already killed the servant and are now attacking the lady herself -- a Spanish knight appears on the scene and, in a fierce struggle, in which he is severely wounded, he succeeds in killing all four highwaymen. The lady brings him to her father's castle, nurses him till he has recovered, and the end, of course, is a wedding party. The fine archaic melody, in alternately 4/4 and 2/4, is pure Dorian. The record gives only the first two stanzas." //CATALOG NUMBER FW03576 / FP 3576 IN FOLKWAYS, RELEVANT INFO IS IN LINER NOTES//
song_lyrics: (Translation) 1. Friends, halt a moment And hear my song That tells you How a beautiful lady Came from the town of Namen That she visited on a pleasure trip. 2. Accompanied by her servant, She was quite unconcerned, But she got in a fearful prrdicament, For, at once, four French highwaymen Sprung out of the bush And assailed this young lady. //CATALOG NUMBER FW03576 / FP 3576 IN FOLKWAYS, RELEVANT INFO IS IN LINER NOTES//

song_info: This song originated in Franche Comt: and its title and first line ran then: "Trois jeunes tambours". From there it came to German speaking countries, and the initial words changed into "Drei sch'one Tamburn". Then it came to the Netherlands, and again the text was translated. The original "jeunes" (young), that changed into the German "schon" (beautiful), became now "schuin,. (equivocal, indecent, naughty). //CATALOG NUMBER FW03576 / FP 3576 IN SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, RELEVANT TEXT IS IN LINER NOTES//
song_lyrics: (Translation) Tlree naughty drummers Came from the East; They came ashore To have a jolly good time And ['ombom, I don't bother, To have a jolly good time, Rombom. One of those three Saw there a nice girl: "Hey, dear lassie, May I be your luver? And rombom, I don't bother, May I be your lover, Rombom. ' "That, young man, You should ask my father. If he agrees, Then I am willing to accept you And rombom, I don't bother, Then I am willing to accept you, Rombom ... "I say, Gov'ner, May I marry your daughter? For, in my eyes, she is The most charming of all women, And rombom, I don't bother, The most charming of all women, Rombom. ,. "Please, young man, Tell me first, what are your possessions, Please, young man, Tell me first, what are your possessions, And"rombom, I don't bother, Tell me first, what are your possessions, Rombom.'· "My possessions are - I will not lie about this - My possessions are A drum and two sticks, And rombom, I don't bother, A drum and two sticks, Rombom." "Well, young man, Then you may not marry my daughter. " "Well, Gov'ner,You can keep her, And rombom, I don't bother, You can keep her, Rombom. '//CATALOG NUMBER FW03576 / FP 3576 IN SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, RELEVANT TEXT IS IN LINER NOTES//

song_info: A well-known 18th century shanty, relating an historical fact. An enterprising and love -sick Dutch girl - whose name has been handed down to us: Grietje van Dijk x) - follows her sweetheart, who happens to be a captain, aboard his ship, unknown to him, and disguised as a sailor. She is ordered to fasten some sails, but being only a frail woman, she does it not all too solidly. So, when the ship meets with foul weather, the sails come down and she is strapped to the mast to be given a thrashing. She cries and tells the men, that she is a girl. The captain recognizes his sweetheart, immediately orders her to be released and, as the text very modestly informs us, "gently touched her with a kiss. " Some few stanzas which are not essential have been left out. The melody given is the most popular Terschelling version; on the "continent" one hears usually another melody, which, ' to my ears, is inferior to the insular one. //CATALOG NUMBER FW03576 / FP 3576 IN SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, RELEVANT INFO IS IN LINER NOTES//

song_lyrics: A large number of the Kjempevise-melodier or ancient heroic ballads have been preserved in the Province of Telemark. The most famous of them all is the so-called Draumkvedet -- the Vision of Heaven and Hell --which dates back to the 12th Century. It describes the legendary dream of a man who slept from Christmas Eve to Epiphany and the visions he wit-nessed of souls in purgatory. Most of these old ballads are in the nature of dance songs in which the leader of the group renders the narrative while others join in the refrain. //SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, Songs and Dances of Norway, CATALOG NUMBER FW04008_207//

song_info: "‘One November evening in 1935, a boat-load of migratory workers, on the last stage of their way home from the Scottish harvest fields, set out from Burtonport to Arranmore. The boat struck a rock in the dark, and the lone survivor of the party was picked up next morning, clinging to an upturned boat, and holding on to the dead body of his father ...’" //IRISH TRADITIONAL MUSIC ARCHIVE, RELEVANT TEXT IS IN THE NOTES FIELD//
song_lyrics: Good people dear, pray lend an ear, I’ll tell you one and all About a great disaster that occurred off Donegal; The wild Atlantic ocean has added to its toll Another nineteen victims: may the Lord receive each soul. 2 ’Twas in the year of thirty-five on a bleak November eve This awful tragedy occurred, it caused us all to grieve; Those cheerful lads returning from the Scottish harvest field Unto the stormy ocean their lives were forced to yield. 3 What cheerful thoughts were in their mind when sailing up Lough Foyle To view the hills of Inishowen, that land of Irish soil! Their little boat came slowly on through Creeslough and Gweedore: Oh God, who’d think they ne’er would reach their native Arranmore! 4 When they arrived at Burtonport they were met upon the pier, They laughed and chatted with their friends all in the best of cheer; They set out for the island but they never reached its shore; A cloud of grief and sorrow hangs over homely Arranmore. 5 Their little boat by God’s will doomed across the waves did sail, There was only one out of a score survived to tell the tale; He saved two other passengers that perished in the cold; The highest praise must be his due, this hero true and bold. 6 So now, kind friends, there’s one request I’ll ask of one and all: Pray for the nineteen victims that were lost off Donegal. With St Patrick and St Bridget may they dwell for ever more In a land where hardships are unknown far away from Arranmore. //IRISH TRADITIONAL MUSIC ARCHIVE, RELEVANT TEXT IS IN THE LYRICS FIELD//

song_lyrics: "One night as I went on my rambles from Swinford to sweet Ballyvee Sure I met a fair maid on my ramble and and her hame it was Mary McGee And she sighed for the rights of her country Michael Davitt her true Irish boy And is now a prisoner of Portland far from the lovely sweet banks of the Moy Don't speak of the sweet sixty seven when brave men were true men you know And the brave Peter Carney God rest him sure he died at Killarney also He was drilled by my darling Mick Davitt in the valleys and glens of Fermoy That's why he's a prisoner of Portland far from the lovely sweet banks of the Moy Well I quickly approached this fair maiden asked her what was the cause of her woe Or what was the cause of her suffering that forced her to leave her own home And she sighed for the rights of her country Michael Davitt her true Irish boy Who is now a prisoner of Portland far from the lovely sweet banks of the Moy Well it’s now to conclude and to finish and I hope that the day will soon come When those cruel landlords and bailiffs from the land of Saint Patrick must run For we’ll unfurl our green and gold banner and to Ireland we’ll raise them on high And we will toast to that brave Michael Davitt on the lovely sweet banks of the Moy." //IRISH TRADITIONAL MUSIC ARCHIVE, THE BANKS OF THE MOY, SONG//

song_info: Salvador da Bahia was founded in 1549 and was the first Portuguese colonial capital. Salvador has retained a strong Afro-Brazilian influence and descendants of African slaves have preserved their heritage in music, food, dance, and martial arts traditions. At festivals, drums beat powerful rhythms and religious followers celebrate African gods at Candomblé ceremonies. Candomblé is a belief system developed by West African slaves based upon Yoruba, Fon, and Bantu beliefs, with Roman Catholic elements. Through singing, drumming, and dancing, worshippers connect with and become possessed by deities called Orishas, who are believed to protect the people and control their destinies. //FOUND IN GLOBAL JUKEBOX "PEOPLE/CULTURE" SECTION OF TRACK DESCRIPTION//

song_info: Tree-felling songs (matjau baai—literally “axe calls”) traditionally accompany this major yearly phase of the horticultural process. In the course of a matjau baai, the singer calls, often repeatedly, on a range of forest gods who have jurisdiction over the site, on other gods and ancestors to give strength to help the work go fast, on still others to bring wind to help blow the tree over, and on others to protect the workers from injury. There is a boastful, arrogant tone in parts of the performance; the men recount their strength, courage and skill, recite their praise names, quote appropriate proverbs, and command the women to fetch water and food. // SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS CATALOGUE NUMBER: FW04225_101, RELEVANT INFO IN LINER NOTES PG. 4 //

song_info: NA
song_lyrics: Remember the arrows he shot from his bow, Remember your chiefs by his hatchet laid low, Why so slow? Do you wait 'till I shrink from the pain? No, the son of Alknomook will never complain. Remember the wood where in ambush we lay, and the scalps which we bore from your nation away, Now the flame rises saft, you exult my pain, But the son of Alknomook can never complain. I go to the land where my father is gone, His ghost shall rejoyce in the fame of his son, Death comes like a friend, he relieves me from pain, And thy son, O Alknomook, has scorn'd to complain //FLUTOPEDIA, RELEVANT INFORMATION FROM THE LINK//

song_info: The Seneca Anthem, although composed in 1976, is based on a melody that Avery Jirrerson learned from his father. Also used in "shows", it is for unaccompanied voice, with a text composed entirely of vocables. The power of the Anthem rests solely on its melody and slow rreasured pace, evoking the quiet dignity and pride of the Senecas //FW04072 / FE 4072 IN SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, RELEVANT INFORMATION IN LINER NOTES//

song_lyrics: The song text reads, approximately: “When Khasan­bek Tavdgiridze became a Turkish general, he forgot God, he angered God. A few Russians also joined our volunteer corps. 9,000 enemy soldiers attacked us. General Eristov sent forth one company which pierced the enemy and beat him. song_label: San Ildefonso: Eagle Dance //CATALOG NUMBER FW04420_103 IN SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, RELEVANT TEXT IS IN THE TITLE FIELD//
song_info: To the American Indian the eagle was a sacred bird venerated for his supernatural power. The eagle dance widely practiced among the pueblos or the southwest reported to be part of an ancient ceremony relating to rain and crops. song_name: Javahé: Sacred-mask-dance, songs, 'Aruana,' Two masks dancing
song_reason: The song is identified as such in its title or label, There is text in associated ethnography that states what type of song this is (this could be in liner notes, it could be in an email from an anthropologist, etc)
song_label: Javahé: Sacred-mask-dance, songs, 'Aruana,' Two masks dancing//SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS CATALOGUE NUMBER: FW04311 / FE 4311//
song_info: Karaja-Indians - About eighthundred Karaja-Indians still live on the shores of the Bananal-Island, Araguaia- River, in Central Brazil. They have been in peaceful contact with our Civilisation since more than a century, but maintain, in spite of, a high level of their old traditions. During the dry season, from May to October, they erect summer-villages of light straw houses on the immense beaches of white, soft and hot sand, performing every afternoon and night their sacred fertility rites, with colorful masks of feather heads and straw skirts. During thesedances they sing old songs of their ancestors, but also new compositions appear from time to time and are traded from one village to another. These'Aruana' masks are stored in special sacred maskhouses far from the row of village houses and are hidden from women. Young men live in these mask houses until they get married. Bachel,prs, who are too lazy to work for their own family live for many years in these mask-houses. Every day, in the late afternoon and during part of the night, pairs of masks start their way from the mask-house to the village, singing and marking rhythm with gourd rattles, they dance close to two waiting women, who then follow them one or two hundred yards through the sand, towards the mask-house but suddenly return to the other women, running, who sit in groups not far from their houses. Javahe-Indians - There are no significant differences between the Karaja and the Javahe, who belong culturally and linguistically to the same group. But the Karaja live on the shores of the Bananal-Island and the Araguaia-River, and the Javahe live inside the huge river-island, being considered the hicks or backwooders. They have less contact with our civilisation and therefore offer in some way better opportunities for the scientist to study certain aspects of their ancient life-style, which maintains less changed than that of the Karaja. About music, the same said for the Karaja is to be repeated for the Javahe, some songs, which are similar in style, are not known by one or the other of both Indian groups, others are common to both of them.//SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS CATALOGUE NUMBER: FW04311 / FE 4311; LINER NOTES (DOWNLOADED PDF)//

song_name: Krahó: Women-and-Men's choir, conducted by traditional choir-leader
song_reason: There is text in associated ethnography that states what type of song this is (this could be in liner notes, it could be in an email from an anthropologist, etc)
song_label: Krahó: Women-and-Men's choir, conducted by traditional choir-leader//SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS CATALOG NUMBER FW04311 / FE 4311//
song_info: Kraho-Indians - About fourhundred and fifty Kraho Indians live in four villages in the wide and open savannah of northeastern Brazil, a few hundred miles from the shores of the Tocantins river, an amazonian tributary. The Kraho belong to the Je-speaking Indians of Brazil, who are by far the most musical and music-loving of all Brazilian Indians. Every morning before sunri.se, one of the beloved and famed choir-conductors starts a dance in middle of the village square, singing with the rhythm of a gourd-rattle. Immediately all younger girls, women and men gather. Women stand in a long row before him, shake the arms rhythmically, whipping their knees in the same rhythm, while men dance in front of them. Songs start slow, heavily, becomes more and more enthusiastic until a powerful choir fills the village at the rising sun. Every afternoon the same happens, after daily work, and every night girls, women and men sing and dance on the village square until dark and late. When everybody is home, resting on their straw-mats spread on the ground or on wooden racks, suddenly a beautiful, sonor voice fills the night - one of the solo-singers of the village performs his recital, some of them with such highly trained voices, that they resemble the educated trembling of coloratur-singers in our society. The Kraho's life is filled with multiple rituals, initiation-rites which change from year to year, being some of them only repeated after twelve up to sixteen years. Each rite is accompanied by special songs, but also the already mentioned morning-and night choir-songs consist of a great number of songs, composed by living choir-leaders or consisting of the traditional repertoire of the tribe. Kraho-life cannot be separated from music! //SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS CATALOGUE NUMBER: FW04311 / FE 4311; LINER NOTES (DOWNLOADED PDF, PP. 4-5)//
song_morenotes: Full recording at Alexander Street: http://ezp-prod1.hul.harvard.edu/login?url=https://search.alexanderstreet.com/view/work/bibliographic_entity|recorded_cd|71744

song_info: This game is a variant of A La Víbora De La Mar, the Spanish equivalent of London Bridge. Two players, holding hands high, join hands to form an arc. Before the game begins, each one selects a secret name. The children pass through the arc one at a time as the song is sung. On the last line one player is detained. The two secret names are whispered to him. He selects one and lines up behind the appropriate person. The game proceeds until everyone has been captured. The game ends with a tug of war. //SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS CATALOG NUMBER FC 7854, LINER NOTES PG. 2//

song_info: During the festival of the communal field, three types of harawi are performed...the first is performed before the young men plow the ground. song_info: "The material presented in this selection consists of dances which are performed to mark the pascola rite of transition from adolescence to adulthood. This ceremony is known as the festival of puberty and is probably one of the most important rituals in their cosmogony as it has associations with the fertility of women and, symbolically, with the fertility of the land. Pascolas are also sung and danced during the Fiesta dedicated by the Seris to the sea turtle, which is an essential part of their diet." //LINER NOTES, PAGE 3: https://folkways-media.si.edu/liner_notes/unesco/UNES08304.pdf//

song_info: This dance tune was played by Juan Hernández, a Tarascan from the village of Santa Fe de la laguna. His small guitar, or jarana, which he called a "guitarra ba:jita, " was designed for nine strings, with four pegs on each side and one in the middle oí the neck. However, Juan had it strung to suit himself, with the last peg in one row omitted. //FOLKWAYS CATALOGUE # FW08867_206, LINER NOTES PAGE 10 , SIDE 2, BAND 6//
song_morenotes: Link to Liner Notes: https://folkways-media.si.edu/liner_notes/folkways/FW08867.pdf

song_label: Dance song//FOLKWAYS CATALOGUE # FW08867_209, LINER NOTES PAGE 11, SIDE 1 , BAND 9//
song_info: This part of the Dance of the Little Old Men, with a Spanish name meaning "Three Little Bells, " is perhaps slightly, more melodious than sorne of the others. However, they are all tune fuI, and such names as "Cruzúcua" (The Cross), "Matóndocua " (Hobbles), or"Serúpacua"(Slide), provide clues concerning the nature of the dance figure or the dancing that accompanies them. Other parts of the dance have such names as "Checánonscua, " (Wheat Tamale), "Canácua" (Crown), and "Tzutzucurápic"(White Flower). //FOLKWAYS CATALOGUE # FW08867_209, LINER NOTES PAGE 11 , SIDE 1 , BAND 9//

song_label: Love//FOLKWAYS CATALOGUE # FW04217_104, LINER NOTES PAGE 5, SIDE 1, BAND 4//
song_info: The Pulido Sisters are a trio of mestizo girls who Sing Tarascan songs. To help with the pronunciation, the composer Juan Victoriano from nearby San Lorenzo, often takes the local bus to Uruapan where the girls live. The~r bright, forceful yet unstrained vocal production. however, is mestizo rather than Indian//FOLKWAYS CATALOGUE # FW04217_104, LINER NOTES PAGE 5, SIDE 1, BAND 4//
song_lyrics: My lovely Reginita, guarecita If you would only give me your love Among the flowers of a thousand colors In a little corner of love//FOLKWAYS CATALOGUE # FW04217_104, LINER NOTES PAGE 5 , SIDE 1 , BAND 4//
morenotes: The liner notes reveal that the singers are mestizo singing Tarascan songs, therefore the extent to which the song can be seen as representing the Tarascan culture can be questioned.

song_info: The qylqobyz, a fiddle with two horsehair strings, which shamans often played, was thought to represent the voice of spirits. It is still considered a sacred instrument, one that brings to a family the protection and barakat (good fate or fortune) of the ancestor who played it. Some qylqobyz players do not let anyone else touch their instrument. //CATALOG NUMBER SFW40438_216 IN SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, RELEVANT TEXT IS IN THE TITLE FIELD//

song_name: You Are the Light of My Eyes
song_reason: The lyrics made it clear to me what type of song this is
song_label: NA
song_info: NA
song_lyrics: You are the light of my eyes, I fell in love with you at first sight. You are the first strike on my heart, come, let’s celebrate life close together. You are the light of my eyes, you are the fragrance of our garden. Oh my love, what is this separation? I have waited enough, my jasmine, You are the light of my eyes. //ON ALEXANDER STREET, IN THE LINER NOTES OF THE ALBUM: Music of Central Asia Vol. 6: Alim and Fargana Qasimov: Spiritual Music of Azerbaijan, PAGE 28, DESCRIPTION OF SONG 8//

song_reason: There is text in associated ethnography that states what type of song this is (this could be in liner notes, it could be in an email from an anthropologist, etc), The lyrics made it clear to me what type of song this is
song_label: NA
song_info: The song tells the story of a young man who falls in love with a neighbor’s daughter and decides to marry her. In order not to burden his poor parents, the lad arranges the marriage himself, and informs his crestfallen mother only after the wedding. //ON ALEXANDER STREET, IN THE LINER NOTES OF THE ALBUM: Music of Central Asia Vol. 6: Alim and Fargana Qasimov: Spiritual Music of Azerbaijan, PAGE 29, DESCRIPTION OF SONG 9//
song_lyrics: My soul has fallen into the love of loves, Mother, my dear, please dry your tears. I have passed twenty years, I cannot wait another year, my dear [mother]. (Refrain) What is this secret I have fallen into, Mother, I have fallen into love’s fire. I never knew the power of love like this before, Mother. //ON ALEXANDER STREET, IN THE LINER NOTES OF THE ALBUM: Music of Central Asia Vol. 6: Alim and Fargana Qasimov: Spiritual Music of Azerbaijan, PAGE 29, DESCRIPTION OF SONG 9//

song_reason: The lyrics made it clear to me what type of song this is

song_info: NA
song_lyrics: Let me encircle you with love, Your black eyes and eyebrows, Jeyran. I have fallen into the flames of longing, Help me to recover from this pain, Jeyran. Let the partridges cackle, my little deer, If I cannot love you openly, Then let me love you in secret. With my saz, with my saz, Oh beloved, I encircle you with love. //ON ALEXANDER STREET, IN THE LINER NOTES OF THE ALBUM: Music of Central Asia Vol. 6: Alim and Fargana Qasimov: Spiritual Music of Azerbaijan, PAGE 30-31, DESCRIPTION OF SONG 8//

song_info: " The Mitote or Xiotal is the most important ritual of the Tepehuanes and is generally performed three times a year to mark the stages in the agricultural cycle, i.e. the dry, rainy and harvest seasons. This ceremony has several different purposes, e.g. to bless the grain of the harvest, ward off evil and bring health and well-being to mankind. This is reflected in the title of one of the dances presented here: the Arrayan or myrtle, which is a Mexican fruit. The enormous importance of the relationship between man and nature in this ritual is reflected in the names of the following pieces : the bull, the water, the feather, the deer, etc." // LINER NOTES, PAGE 5: https://folkways-media.si.edu/liner_notes/unesco/UNES08304.pdf//

song_info: "Kick-ball races occupy a prominent place in the games of the Tarahumares. and in Sierra communities they are held every Sunday. It is a common sight in the Sierra to see men and boys practicing for the races by kicking balls made of the madrono wood as they run along the trails. It is the ambition of every Tarahumar youth to be a great runner, and this song tells the simple story of the little boy who wanted to race up the hill with his kick-ball" //IN LINER NOTES, PAGE 9: https://folkways-media.si.edu/liner_notes/folkways/FW08867.pdf //

song_label: Zuni: Rain Dance //CATALOG NUMBER FW04420_104 IN SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, RELEVANT TEXT IS IN THE TITLE FIELD//
song_info: The making of the rain is of the primary concern in pueblo religions and most of the ceremonies are directed toward that objective. The dance like their ritual poetry is a monotonous compulsion of natural forces by reiteration. the Tier-less pounding of their feet draws together the mist in the sky and heaps it into the piled rain clouds. It forces out the rain upon the earth. They are bent not at all upon an ecstatic experience, but upon so through-going an identification with nature that the forces of nature will swing to their purposes. //CATALOG NUMBER FW420_104 IN SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, RELEVANT TEXT IS IN LINAR NOTES//

song_lyrics: "Destroy the friendship with TB by avoiding smoking and alcoholic drinks How can you avoid this pulmonary tuberculosis?
